examples.text: وَرَدَتْنِي عُرُوضٌ مِنْ كُلِّ أَنْحَاءِ ٱلْعَالَمِ.
examples.english: Offers from all over the world came to me.

senses.gloss: to be said, to be mentioned

examples.text: أُرِيدُ أَنْ أَتَعَرَّفَ إِلَى أَبْرَزِ مَا وَرَدَ فِي الصِّحَافَةِ الدَّوْلِيَّةِ.
examples.english: I want to find out the most important things reported by the international press.

senses.gloss: (esp. in plural) specified time of day or night devoted to private prayer

examples.text: الْوَرْدُ، الَّذِي قُبِلَ بِنَدَى الصَّبَاحِ، هَمَسَ أَسْرَارَ الْحُبِّ وَالْجَمَالِ لِلْعَالَمِ، وَزُهُورُهُ الرَّقِيقَةُ شَاهِدَةٌ عَلَى هَشَاشَةِ الْحَيَاةِ.
examples.english: The rose, kissed by the morning dew, whispered secrets of love and beauty to the world, its delicate petals a testament to the fragility of life.

senses.gloss: to blossom, to be in bloom
to dye or paint red, to rouge

etymology.text:
From Proto-Semitic *warad-. Cognate with Hebrew יָרַד (yārád, “to go down”), Aramaic ירד (yreḏ, “stream”), Ge'ez ወረደ (wärädä, “fall down, go down, drain off, pour down”), and Akkadian 𒉿𒊏𒁺𒌝 (warādum, “to go down to lower ground, to come ashore, to go downhill or downstream”) and Ugaritic 𐎊𐎗𐎄 (yrd).

As Aramaic וַרְדָּא (wardā), ܘܪܕܐ (wardā), Hebrew וֶרֶד (wéreḏ), and Old Armenian վարդ (vard) and Ancient Greek ϝρόδον (wródon), borrowed from a reflex of Proto-Iranian *wardah. Doublet of جُلّ (jull).

Denominal verb of وَرْد (ward, “roses, blossoms”).
